title: Dyeing with oak galls
subtitle: Using dried oak galls as a tannin mordant and grey-black dye on protein and cellulose fibres
excerpt: Oak galls are abnormal growths on oak trees containing high concentrations of tannic acid. They serve two roles in natural dyeing: as a tannin pre-mordant that helps metallic mordants bond to cellulose fibres such as cotton and linen, and as a dye in their own right that produces pale beige on alum-mordanted wool and a strong grey-black when combined with iron. This guide covers both uses.
sourceNotes: Pellew, Charles N. 'Dyes and Dyeing.' McBride, Nast, 1913. Chapter IV covers tannin mordants and gall-iron combinations. Internet Archive: archive.org/details/dyesdyeing00pellrich. Gall-iron reactions are standard historical dye practice.

attrs.body: Wear rubber gloves when handling iron sulphate solution. Use dedicated pots not used for food.
attrs.heading: Safety

content.text: . On alum-mordanted wool, the gall bath produces a warm beige. Combined with iron sulphate in a
sequence, the same fibre produces a deep grey-black: one of the strongest colours achievable from plant sources.

items.name: Iron sulphate crystals (for gall-iron black only)
Stainless steel or enamel dye pot (dedicated)

content.text: Place the crushed oak galls in the dye pot with enough cold water to cover generously. Bring slowly to the boil, then simmer for 45-60 minutes. Strain out the gall pieces and return the liquid to the pot. The bath will be a dark brown-amber colour.
Step 2: Dye the fibre in the gall bath

Add the pre-wetted, alum-mordanted fibre to the warm gall bath. Raise to 80-85 degrees Celsius over 30 minutes, then hold at that temperature for a further 30-45 minutes. For a beige-yellow result on wool, this step is complete. Remove the fibre, squeeze gently, and rinse. For the gall-iron black, proceed to the next step.

Dissolve 4-6 g of iron sulphate crystals in a cup of hot water, add to a fresh pot of warm water, and stir well. Add the still-damp gall-dyed fibre. Heat to 60-70 degrees Celsius and hold for 20-30 minutes. The fibre will turn from beige to dark grey-black as the tannin and iron react on the fibre surface. Remove and rinse well.

Rinse the dyed fibre thoroughly until the water runs clear. Iron can make wool feel stiff: a brief soak in a small amount of hair conditioner diluted in warm water restores softness. Rinse again, roll in a towel, and dry away from direct sunlight.

items.fix: Keep the fibre submerged with a lid or weight and stir gently every 5 minutes. Uneven contact with the iron solution causes uneven take-up.
items.cause: Fibre not fully submerged in the iron bath, or agitated too rarely
items.problem: The grey-black result is patchy or uneven

items.fix: Keep iron sulphate at 4 g per 100 g fibre. Limit the iron bath to 30 minutes maximum. Follow with a conditioner rinse.
items.cause: Iron concentration too high, or fibre left in the iron bath too long
items.problem: The wool feels harsh and scratchy after dyeing

items.fix: For cellulose fibres, a gall bath serves as the tannin pre-mordant step. After the gall bath, follow with an alum mordant bath, then dye. Do not skip the gall stage on cotton.
items.cause: Cotton not pre-treated with a tannin mordant before the metallic mordant
items.problem: Cotton takes no colour from the gall bath
